title: Alternate Rebellion and Adaptive Denial Alternate Rebellion

Distress Tolerance Handout 21   (Distress Tolerance Worksheet 18)
Alternate Rebellion and Adaptive Denial
Alternate Rebellion
When addictive behaviors are a way to rebel against authority, conventions, and the boredom
of not breaking rules or laws, try alternate rebellion. Alternate rebellion replaces destructive
rebellion and keeps you on a path to your goals.
Examples:

Â‰ Shave your head.

Â‰ Wear crazy underwear.

Â‰ Wear unmatched shoes.

Â‰ Have secret thoughts.

Â‰ Express unpopular views.

Â‰ Do random acts of kindness.

Â‰ Vacation with your family at a nudist colony.

Â‰ Write a letter saying exactly what you want to.

Â‰ Dye your hair a wild color.

Â‰ Get a tattoo or body piercing.

Â‰ Wear clothes inside out.

Â‰ Donâ€™t bathe for a week.

Â‰ Print a slogan on a t-shirt.

Â‰ Paint your face.

Â‰ Dress up or dress down where
doing so is unexpected.
Adaptive Denial
When your mind canâ€™t tolerate craving for addictive behaviors, try adaptive denial.

Â‰ Give logic a break when you are doing this. Donâ€™t argue with yourself.

Â‰ When urges hit, deny that you want the problem behavior or substance. Convince yourself
you want something other than the problem behavior. For example, reframe an urge to
have a cigarette as an urge to have a flavored toothpick; an urge to have alcohol as an
urge to have something sweet; or an urge to gamble as an urge to alternate rebellion (see
above).
Other:

Other:

Be adamant with yourself in your denial, and engage in the alternative behavior.

Â‰ Put off addictive behavior. Put it off for 5 minutes, then put it off for another 5 minutes, and
so on and on, each time saying, â€śI only have to stand this for 5 minutes.â€ť By telling yourself
each day you will be abstinent for today (or each hour for just this hour, and so on), you are
saying, â€śThis is not forever. I can stand this right now.â€ť

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Alternate Rebellion and Adaptive Denial Alternate Rebellion
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Distress tolerance means getting through the wave without adding harm. Use gentle, medically safer versions first.
tiny_steps: Choose one body-safe skill for 30 seconds.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Replace intense exercise with hand squeeze, paced exhale, safe fidget, weighted comfort if tolerated, or visual grounding.
Avoid cold shock/breath-holding/intense exercise unless already clinician-approved and safe that day.
Keep oxygen, rescue meds, allergy plan, mobility aids, and exit routes accessible.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not use crisis skills as punishment, sensory flooding, or exposure without consent.
